1: Now the famine was still severe in the land — the famine that began in Genesis 41 presses the covenant family again. The “severe famine” is the providential pressure that drives Jacob’s sons back to Egypt — back to Joseph. Without the famine, there is no second journey; without the second journey, there is no revelation of Joseph. The suffering that drives the sinner back to the Savior is one of the consistent patterns of the OT’s theology of hardship: “He humbled you, causing you to hunger and then feeding you with manna” (Deuteronomy 8:3); “Before I was afflicted I went astray, but now I obey your word” (Psalms 119:67). Christ identifies himself as the bread for the spiritually famished: “I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry” (John 6:35). The famine that drives the brothers back to Joseph is the type of the spiritual hunger that drives the sinner to Christ.
2: When they had eaten all the grain they had brought from Egypt, their father said to them, “Go back and buy us a little more food” — the exhaustion of the first provision compels the return journey. The first supply ran out; a second journey is required. The once-and-for-all provision that Christ gives contrasts with the repeated returns required by temporary provision: “everyone who drinks this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst” (John 4:13–14). The grain from Egypt that runs out after one season is the type of the law’s temporary provision — adequate for the moment, demanding return. Christ gives the living water and the bread of life that does not run out, that satisfies permanently (John 6:51: “this bread… whoever eats this bread will live forever”).
3: Judah said to him, “The man warned us solemnly, ‘You will not see my face again unless your brother is with you’” — Joseph’s condition: the face of the one in authority cannot be seen without the younger brother present. The condition of access to the ruler’s face is the presence of Benjamin — the “son of the right hand,” the beloved younger brother. Christ is the condition of access to the Father: “No one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6). The brothers cannot see Joseph’s face without Benjamin; humanity cannot see the Father’s face without Christ. The condition of access to the divine presence is the presence of the beloved Son.
4: “If you will send our brother along with us, we will go down and buy food for you” — the condition of the second journey: Benjamin must come. The covenant-bearer who must accompany the brothers for their mission to succeed is the type of Christ’s indispensable presence in the church’s mission: “Apart from me you can do nothing” (John 15:5). The disciples’ mission succeeds only “with him” — “They went out and preached everywhere, and the Lord worked with them” (Mark 16:20). Benjamin’s presence as the condition of the mission’s success is the type of Christ’s accompanying presence that makes the church’s mission fruitful.
5: “But if you will not send him, we will not go down, because the man said to us, ‘You will not see my face unless your brother is with you’” — the repeated emphasis on the condition of access. The binary: with Benjamin, access is possible; without Benjamin, no access. The exclusivity of the access-condition is not cruelty but the logic of the revelation that is coming: Joseph’s revelation of himself requires Benjamin’s presence because the revelation concerns the whole family. The gospel’s exclusive claim — “Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved” (Acts 4:12) — is not cruelty but the logic of a specific revelation: God reconciles the world to himself in this particular way, through this particular person.
6: Israel asked, “Why did you bring this trouble on me by telling the man you had another brother?” — Jacob’s frustration at the situation: the disclosure that they had another brother has created the conditions that now demand Benjamin’s journey. The truth that was disclosed creates the obligation to fulfill the whole truth. Once the brothers acknowledged Benjamin’s existence, they could not keep him hidden. The covenant community’s transparency about the full truth of its identity — not concealing any part of its witness — is the same principle. Paul: “we have renounced secret and shameful ways; we do not use deception, nor do we distort the word of God. On the contrary, by setting forth the truth plainly we commend ourselves to everyone’s conscience” (2 Corinthians 4:2). The full disclosure creates obligations; honesty about Christ means full representation of his claims.
7: They replied, “The man questioned us closely about ourselves and our family. ‘Is your father still living?’ he asked us. ‘Do you have another brother?’” — Joseph’s questions about the father and the family. The one in authority who asks about the father and the family is the type of Christ who “knows what is in each person” (John 2:25) — who asks about the heart of the situation. “Is your father still living?” — the question about the covenant patriarch — is the question about the continuity of the covenant relationship. Christ’s question “Who do you say I am?” (Matthew 16:15) is the equivalent question that reveals the heart’s true relationship to the covenant.
8: Then Judah said to Israel his father, “Send the boy along with me and we will go at once, so that we and you and our children may live and not die” — Judah’s appeal in the language of life and death: “so that we and you and our children may live and not die.” The urgency of covenant life and death is the urgency of the gospel: “Truly I tell you, unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you” (John 6:53). The choice between going to the one who has provision (at the cost of the beloved son’s risk) or staying and dying in the famine is the choice between faith and unbelief. Judah sees clearly: the alternative to the journey is death. The gospel’s urgency is not excessive; the alternative to coming to Christ is spiritual famine and death.
9: “I myself will guarantee his safety; you can hold me personally responsible for him. If I do not bring him back to you and set him here before you, I will bear the blame before you all my life” — Judah’s surety: he takes personal responsibility for Benjamin’s safety, permanently. The guarantee is total: “I will bear the blame before you all my life.” Hebrews 7:22: “Jesus has become the guarantor of a better covenant.” Christ is the eggys — the legal guarantor — of the new covenant. Judah’s personal surety for Benjamin (the son of the right hand) is the type of Christ’s absolute guarantee: he stands between the believer and all that would harm them, taking personal and permanent responsibility. The one who can say “no one will snatch them out of my hand” (John 10:28) is the guarantor.
10: “As it is, if we had not delayed, we could have gone and returned twice” — Judah’s pragmatic note: the delay has cost them. The delay in coming to the one who has provision is costly. The parable of the ten virgins: “the door was shut” for those who delayed (Matthew 25:10–12). Paul: “now is the time of God’s favor, now is the day of salvation” (2 Corinthians 6:2). The urgency of not delaying the response to the one who has provision runs from Judah’s pragmatic observation to the gospel’s eschatological urgency. The delay that the family experienced — and the unnecessary suffering that followed — is the type of the unnecessary delay of faith that costs more than it saves.
11: Their father Israel said to them, “If it must be, then do this: Put some of the best products of the land in your bags and take them down to the man as a gift” — Jacob’s gift of the best products of the land to Joseph (unknowingly). The covenant father sends the best of the covenant land as a gift to the son who is lord of Egypt. The irony is profound: Jacob sends gifts to the son who has everything, not knowing that Joseph already has more than these gifts could provide. The gifts humans bring to God — “the best products of the land” — are received not because God needs them but because the giving is an act of the giver’s faith and love. “God loves a cheerful giver” (2 Corinthians 9:7); the gift is not for the recipient’s need but for the giver’s blessing.
12: “Take double the amount of silver with you, for you must return the silver that was put back into the mouths of your sacks. Perhaps it was a mistake” — Jacob sends double silver: one payment for the new grain, one to return what was mysteriously returned to them. The “perhaps it was a mistake” reflects Jacob’s honest uncertainty about a grace he cannot explain. The mysterious silver returned to the sacks — which was grace from Joseph — is treated as potential error to be corrected. The human tendency to explain away grace as accident or mistake is the perpetual problem: “We saw no miracle” (Matthew 13:58). Jacob cannot interpret the grace correctly without knowing the giver. Christ’s grace is correctly received only when the Giver is known.
13: “Take your brother also and go back to the man at once” — the final instruction: take Benjamin and go immediately. The decisive moment of surrender — the father releases the beloved son into the journey that could be dangerous. “God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son” (John 3:16). The father who releases the beloved son to the journey that is necessary for others’ salvation is the type of the heavenly Father’s giving of the Son. Jacob’s “Take your brother also and go” is the covenant patriarch’s participation in the divine pattern: the beloved son is given for the family’s life.
14: “And may God Almighty grant you mercy before the man so that he will let your other brother and Benjamin come back with you. As for me, if I am bereaved, I am bereaved” — Jacob’s prayer to El Shaddai — the covenant name of God Almighty — and his surrender: “if I am bereaved, I am bereaved.” The covenant prayer that surrenders to the outcome — the prayer that asks and then releases — is the pattern of Christ’s Gethsemane prayer: “Not as I will, but as you will” (Matthew 26:39). The father’s surrender of the beloved son to the journey with the prayer for mercy is the type of the Father’s surrender of the Son: “He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all” (Romans 8:32). “If I am bereaved, I am bereaved” is the covenant surrender of the one who trusts God with what he loves most.
15: So the men took the gifts and double the amount of silver, and Benjamin also. They hurried down to Egypt and presented themselves to Joseph — the journey executed: the gifts, the silver, Benjamin. The presentation of themselves to Joseph — the one who has authority over their lives — is the type of the sinner presenting himself to Christ. “Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved” (Romans 10:13). The brothers who had rejected Joseph (Genesis 37:18–20) now present themselves to him. The gospel’s invitation is precisely to those who have rejected the one they now approach: “we were God’s enemies” (Romans 5:10) who come to the one who reconciles. The journey from rejection to presentation is the arc of repentance and faith.
16: When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the steward of his house, “Take these men to my house, slaughter an animal and prepare dinner, for these men are to eat with me at noon” — Joseph’s response to seeing Benjamin: immediate hospitality. The sight of the beloved brother provokes not judgment but a feast. The table prepared for those who had wronged him is Psalms 23:5 in narrative form: “You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies.” Christ’s response to repentance is the feast: the father in the prodigal parable orders the fatted calf slaughtered (Luke 15:23). The meal with the one who has authority over life and death is the type of the eschatological banquet: “Blessed are those who are invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb!” (Revelation 19:9).
17: The man did as Joseph told him and took the men to Joseph’s house — the faithful steward who executes the master’s hospitality is the type of the Spirit who brings believers to the Father: “No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws them” (John 6:44); “the Spirit of God leads them” (Romans 8:14). The divine initiative — the master who commands, the steward who executes, the guests who arrive — is the pattern of salvation: not the guests’ initiative but the master’s. “You did not choose me, but I chose you” (John 15:16).
18: Now the men were frightened when they were taken to his house. They thought, “We were brought here because of the silver that was put back into our sacks” — the brothers interpret the unexpected hospitality as accusation. What is actually grace looks to them like a trap. The misinterpretation of grace as accusation is the sinner’s consistent first response: “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord” (Luke 5:8). The one who is unaccustomed to grace tends to experience it as judgment. Christ comes as grace but is perceived as threat by those whose conscience is active: “the light has come into the world, but people loved darkness instead of light because their deeds were evil” (John 3:19). The gospel must be heard as “good news” — not merely as the arrival of the one who knows all about us.
19: So they went up to Joseph’s steward and spoke to him at the entrance to the house — the brothers preemptively explain themselves to the steward before entering. The defensive explanation before the potential accusation — getting ahead of the charge — is the guilty conscience’s strategy. The one who is truly forgiven does not need to preemptively explain; the gospel’s assurance releases the need for defensive self-justification. “There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1). The brothers who stand at the entrance with their prepared explanation are the type of those who approach God with prepared excuses; the gospel’s answer is the steward’s: “It’s all right… don’t be afraid” (v23).
20: “We beg your pardon, our lord, we came down here the first time to buy food” — the brothers begin their prepared explanation at the steward. The confession of the original purpose (buying food) — innocent and legitimate — is the foundation of their explanation. The honest account of one’s actual situation before God — “I came because I was hungry, because I needed” — is the beginning of honest prayer. The Psalms of lament are often this: a frank account of the actual need without pretense (Psalms 22:1–2: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”). Christ teaches direct petition: “Give us today our daily bread” (Matthew 6:11) — the simple, honest statement of need.
21: “But at the place where we stopped for the night we opened our sacks and each of us found his silver — the exact weight — in the mouth of his sack” — the brothers report the mysterious return of the silver. “The exact weight” — nothing was missing from the original payment; the grace was complete and precise. The exactness of the divine grace — not approximately sufficient but exactly adequate — is the pattern of the atonement’s precision: “The wages of sin is death” (Romans 6:23) — exact; “he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him” (Hebrews 5:9) — complete. The exact weight of silver that was returned prefigures the exact payment that Christ makes: “He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross” (1 Peter 2:24).
22: “And we have brought additional silver with us to buy food. We don’t know who put our silver in our sacks” — the brothers’ honest admission of ignorance. They bring double silver because they don’t know how to account for the grace — whether it was error or gift — and want to be prepared for either interpretation. The uncertainty about whether a gift is truly a gift — “we don’t know who” — is the state before the full revelation. When Joseph reveals himself (Genesis 45), the mystery is resolved: “I am Joseph!” resolves the enigma of all the grace that preceded it. The full revelation of Christ makes sense of all the grace that preceded it: the OT’s mysterious grace (the sacrificial system, the covenant, the manna) is resolved when “the Word became flesh” (John 1:14).
23: “It’s all right,” he said. “Don’t be afraid. Your God, the God of your father, has given you treasure in your sacks; I received your payment.” — the steward’s remarkable response. He names the returned silver as “treasure” from “your God, the God of your father.” The accusation feared is transformed into a declaration of divine grace. “Don’t be afraid” is one of the NT’s most frequent divine announcements (the angels at the tomb: “Do not be afraid”; Jesus: “Do not be afraid”; Revelation 1:17). The steward reframes what the brothers understood as evidence of guilt (the silver) as the gift of God. The gospel’s reframing: what we thought was evidence of our condemnation (Christ’s cross — the sign of criminal execution) is declared the instrument of God’s mercy. The cross as treasure, not accusation.
24: The steward took the men into Joseph’s house, gave them water to wash their feet and provided fodder for their donkeys — the comprehensive hospitality of the household: water for feet, fodder for animals. The comprehensive hospitality that attends to both the guests and their animals is the type of Christ’s comprehensive provision. “Your Father knows what you need before you ask him” (Matthew 6:8); “my God will meet all your needs according to the riches of his glory in Christ Jesus” (Philippians 4:19). The household that receives the guests and provides for all their needs — not just the spiritual but the practical — is the type of the kingdom community that attends to the whole person. The foot-washing by the steward prefigures Christ’s foot-washing in John 13 as an act of hospitality and humility.
25: They prepared their gifts for Joseph’s arrival at noon, because they had heard that they were to eat there — the brothers prepare their gifts in anticipation of Joseph’s arrival. The preparation that precedes the encounter with the ruler is the type of the preparatory posture of worship. The Psalms of ascent (Ps 120–134) prepare the pilgrim for the encounter at Jerusalem. Christ prepares his disciples for his “arrival” — the second coming — by teaching them to be ready: “Therefore keep watch, because you do not know on what day your Lord will come” (Matthew 24:42). The gifts prepared for Joseph’s arrival are the type of the “righteous acts of God’s holy people” (Revelation 19:8) that the church brings to the Lamb’s wedding feast.
26: When Joseph came home, they presented to him the gifts they had brought inside, and they bowed down before him to the ground — the bowing before Joseph fulfills his early dreams (Genesis 37:7–9: the sheaves bowing; the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing). The brothers who rejected the dreamer now bow before the ruler — the dream fulfilled exactly as the rejected one had dreamed it. The fulfillment of the prophetic vision through the very people who rejected it is the pattern of the Palm Sunday acclaim and the ultimate eschatological fulfillment: “They will look on me, the one they have pierced, and they will mourn” (Zechariah 12:10; Revelation 1:7). Every knee bows before the one who was rejected (Philippians 2:10–11): the fulfillment of the prophetic vision through those who opposed it.
27: He asked them how they were, and then he said, “How is your aged father you told me about? Is he still living?” — Joseph’s genuine pastoral inquiry about the father. The one who rules asks not about his own honor (though they have bowed to him) but about the wellbeing of the absent father and family. The shepherd-ruler who asks after the flock rather than after his own glory is the type of Christ’s pastoral heart: “He asked them, ‘How many loaves do you have?’” (Mark 6:38); “When Jesus looked up and saw a great crowd coming toward him, he said to Philip, ‘Where shall we buy bread for these people to eat?’” (John 6:5). The one in authority whose first question is about others’ wellbeing rather than his own is the type of the servant-Lord.
28: They replied, “Your servant our father is still alive and well.” And they bowed down, prostrating themselves before him — the double bowing — first at entry (v26), again at the mention of the father. The reflexive posture of reverence before the one who holds authority is the type of the worship that the presence of Christ evokes: “When they saw him, they worshiped him” (Matthew 28:17); Thomas: “My Lord and my God!” (John 20:28). The news that “your servant our father is still alive and well” is the news about the covenant patriarch’s continuation — the continuity of the covenant family despite all the suffering. Christ guarantees the Father’s covenant family’s continuation: “I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it” (Matthew 16:18).
29: As he looked about and saw his brother Benjamin, his own mother’s son, he asked, “Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?” And he said, “God be gracious to you, my son” — Joseph sees Benjamin — his only full brother, his mother Rachel’s other son — and blesses him. “God be gracious to you” is the covenant blessing: the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:25 (“the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you”) in Joseph’s mouth. The ruler who blesses the beloved younger brother with the covenant blessing is the type of Christ blessing his disciples: “While he was blessing them, he left them and was taken up into heaven” (Luke 24:51). The final action of the ascended Christ was blessing — the covenant grace poured out on those he loves.
30: Deeply moved at the sight of his brother, Joseph hurried out and looked for a place to weep. He went into his private room and wept there — Joseph’s overwhelming emotion at seeing Benjamin. The ruler who weeps privately — who maintains composure in public but is undone by love for his family — is the type of Christ’s emotional depth. “Jesus wept” (John 11:35) — the most concentrated emotional display in the Gospels, also at a family reunion: Lazarus raised. “He was deeply moved in spirit and troubled” (John 11:33). The one who holds authority is not emotionally invulnerable; the depth of his love makes him susceptible to tears. The sovereign who weeps is the type of the God who “in all their distress he too was distressed” (Isaiah 63:9).
31: After washing his face, he came out and, controlling himself, said, “Serve the food” — Joseph’s self-mastery: he washes, controls himself, and returns to public composure. The ruler who has been undone by love but gathers himself to provide for his family is the type of Christ’s purposeful self-control in his passion: “He set his face toward Jerusalem” (Luke 9:51); “for the joy set before him he endured the cross” (Hebrews 12:2). The composure that follows the private tears is the composure of the one who knows what must be done and does it despite the emotional cost. “Serve the food” — the host who provides for those who have harmed him — is the type of Christ who feeds his disciples at the Last Supper: “Jesus knew that the Father had put all things under his power… so he got up from the meal, took off his outer clothing, and wrapped a towel around his waist” (John 13:3–4).
32: They served him by himself, the brothers by themselves, and the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves, because Egyptians could not eat with Hebrews — the segregated table: three groups, each eating separately according to Egyptian custom. The social barriers that prevent full table-fellowship — “Egyptians could not eat with Hebrews” — are the type of the social/ethnic/religious barriers that Christ breaks down. Galatians 3:28: “There is neither Jew nor Gentile… you are all one in Christ Jesus.” The one table of the Lord’s Supper replaces the segregated tables of the old order: “For whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes” (1 Corinthians 11:26). The table at which all eat together — the eschatological feast — replaces the segregated meal of Joseph’s Egypt.
33: The men had been seated before him in the order of their ages, from the firstborn to the youngest; and they looked at each other in astonishment — the brothers seated in perfect birth order by Joseph’s knowledge of their family. The one who knows the exact family order without being told is the one who knows what is in each person (John 2:25). “You perceive my thoughts from afar… you are familiar with all my ways” (Psalms 139:2–3). The astonishment of being seated by someone who knows your exact place — who knows more about you than you expect — is the type of the encounter with the omniscient Christ who addresses each person by name (John 10:3: “He calls his own sheep by name”). The perfect knowledge of the one who arranges is the sign of divine authority over human ordering.
34: When portions of food were served to them from Joseph’s table, Benjamin’s portion was five times as much as anyone else’s. So they feasted and drank freely with him — the abundant feast and Benjamin’s five-fold portion. Benjamin — the “son of the right hand” — receives five times the abundance of the others. The covenant-fulfillment that gives “abundantly more than all we ask or imagine” (Ephesians 3:20) is the type of Christ’s superabundant provision for the beloved. The feast that ends the chapter — “they feasted and drank freely with him” — is the OT feast of reconciliation that anticipates the eschatological feast: “Then the master told his servant, ‘Go out to the roads and country lanes and compel them to come in, so that my house will be full’” (Luke 14:23).
